origin.question: Problem 11.6. Two bodies A and B have momenta $\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{A}}$ and $\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{B}}$ and spin $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{A}}$ and $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{B}}$ ; their centers of mass are on a collision course. After colliding they stick together to form a composite body C (spin $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{C}}$ ). Calculate $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{C}}$ in terms of $\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{A}}, \mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{B}}, \mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{A}}$ and $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{B}}$ .
origin.answer: $\\mathbf{S}_C^\\sigma = - S_A^\\sigma (u_A \\cdot u_C) - S_B^\\sigma (u_B \\cdot u_C) + u_C^\\sigma (S_A \\cdot u_A + S_B \\cdot u_B)$, with $\\mathbf{u} = (\\mathbf{P}_A + \\mathbf{P}_B)/|\\mathbf{P}_A + \\mathbf{P}_B|$
origin.solution:
Solution 11.6. First we calculate the total angular momentum. We work in the center-of-momentum frame and take as our origin 0 the event at which the center of mass of A and that of B would collide. About this origin both systems A and B have only intrinsic angular momentum (see Problem 11.5), so
$$
\mathbf {J}_{\mathbf {A} + \mathbf {B}}^{\alpha \beta} (\mathbf {0}) = \mathbf {S}_{\mathbf {A}}^{\alpha \beta} + \mathbf {S}_{\mathbf {B}}^{\alpha \beta} = - \varepsilon^{\alpha \beta \mu \nu} (\mathbf {S}_{\mathbf {A}^{\mu} \mathbf {A}^{\nu}} + \mathbf {S}_{\mathbf {B}^{\mu} \mathbf {B}^{\nu}})
$$
and by conservation of angular momentum this is equal to $\int_{\mathbf{C}}^{\alpha  eta} (\mathbf{0}) \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{C}$ . We can now use this result in the definition of the spin vector to find
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {\Delta}_{\mathbf {C}^{\sigma}} = - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_{\sigma a \beta \lambda} \mathbf {\Delta}_{\mathbf {C}}^{a \beta} \mathbf {\Delta}_{\mathbf {C}}^{\mathbf {u}^{\lambda}} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_{\sigma \alpha \beta \lambda} \varepsilon^{\alpha \beta \mu \nu}_{\mathbf {A}^{\mu} \mathbf {A}^{\nu}} (\mathbf {S}_{\mu} \mathbf {u}_{\nu} + \mathbf {S}_{\mu} \mathbf {\Gamma}_{\mathbf {B}} \mathbf {u}_{\nu}) \mathbf {\Gamma}_{\mathbf {C}}^{\lambda}. \\ \end{array}
$$
According to Problem 3.28 (see also Problem 3.27)
$$
\varepsilon_{\sigma \alpha \beta \lambda} \varepsilon^{\alpha \beta \mu \nu} = - 2 \delta_{\sigma \lambda}^{\mu \nu}
$$
so that
$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathop{\rm S}\lim its_{\mathbf{C}}\sigma = -\delta^{\mu \nu}_{\sigma \lambda}(\mathop{\rm S}\lim its_{\mathbf{A}}{}^{\mu}\mathop{\rm u}\lim its_{\mathbf{A}} + \mathop{\rm S}\lim its_{\mathbf{B}}{}^{\mu}\mathop{\rm u}\lim its_{\mathbf{B}})^{\mathsf{u}^{\lambda}}_{\mathbf{C}} \\ = - \mathrm {S}_{\sigma} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}) - \mathrm {S}_{\sigma} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}) + \mathrm {u}_{\sigma} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u}) + \mathrm {u}_{\sigma} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u}) . \\ \end{array}
$$
We can now substitute $ rac{(\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P})}{|\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}|}$ for $\mathbf{u}$ to complete the solution.
